title: How to approach parts that do not trust the therapeutic process
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

The part that crosses its arms and says "I don't trust this"

htmlContent:
The part that crosses its arms and says "I don't trust this"

I've been there. Sitting across from a therapist, or staring at a workbook, or listening to someone tell me that "healing is possible." And inside me, there's this part that just... scoffs. Rolls its eyes. Crosses its arms so tight I can feel the tension in my shoulders. That part has a lot to say. Mostly variations of "This is stupid," "They don't get it," and "Nothing ever works anyway."

Here's the thing. That part is not wrong. It's not broken. It's not the enemy of your healing. It's a protector. A very tired, very wary protector that has been doing its job for a very long time. And it deserves your respect, not your frustration.

When I first started working with parts that don't trust the therapeutic process, I wanted to shove them aside. I wanted them to shut up so I could "get better." But that's not how this works. You can't bully a protector into relaxing. You have to understand why it's so scared.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, spent decades studying how the nervous system responds to threat. He found that when we experience trauma, our survival responses get stuck. The part that doesn't trust therapy? It's stuck in a survival response. It remembers every time someone promised to help and then hurt you. It remembers every time vulnerability led to betrayal. It's not being difficult. It's being vigilant.

Why your protector is so damn good at its job

Think about it. This part of you has kept you alive. It has kept you safe from people who would exploit your openness. It has saved you from repeating the same painful patterns. It has a track record. And it's not about to let some therapist or self-help book undo all that hard work.

I remember working with a part that flat-out refused to engage with any healing modality. Every time I tried to do a grounding exercise, it would make me feel drowsy. Every time I tried to talk about a traumatic memory, it would give me a headache. Every time I tried to meditate, it would fill my mind with racing thoughts. I thought I was broken. I thought I couldn't heal. But Janina Fisher, a leading expert in trauma treatment, would call this a classic protector response. She talks about how parts often use physical symptoms to keep us from getting too close to the pain.

Your protector is not trying to ruin your life. It's trying to prevent you from being retraumatized. And honestly, it has good reason to be suspicious. The wellness industry is full of people who promise transformation without understanding trauma. Toxic positivity tells you to "just think positive" when your nervous system is screaming. The part that doesn't trust this? It's the only one paying attention.

So here's what I've learned to do. Instead of fighting that part, I thank it. I say, "I see you. I hear you. You've worked so hard to keep me safe. Thank you." And you know what happens? It doesn't immediately relax. It doesn't trust me either. But it stops fighting quite so hard. It starts to listen.

Building trust with the part that doesn't trust

Trust is not built overnight. Especially not with a part that has been burned before. You don't get to walk in and demand trust. You earn it. Slowly. Painfully. One small moment at a time.

I start by asking the part what it needs. Not what I think it needs. What it actually needs. And I listen. I don't argue. I don't try to convince it that therapy is safe. I just let it talk. Sometimes it says things like "I need you to stop pushing." Sometimes it says "I need you to acknowledge that this might not work." Sometimes it just stays silent. That's okay. Silence is a form of communication too.

A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection is Radical Acceptance (paid link). Tara Brach's work on radical acceptance has been incredibly helpful for me in learning to for the parts that feel unworthy of healing. The idea is simple but : you don't have to like the part. You don't have to agree with it. You just have to accept that it's there, doing its best to protect you.

When I practice radical acceptance with my protectors, I notice something shifts. The part feels seen. It feels heard. And when a part feels seen and heard, it doesn't have to scream so loud. It can start to relax, just a little. Just enough to let me see what's underneath.

Stay with me here. Because what's underneath is often the most tender, wounded part of you. The part that was hurt so badly that the protector had to step in. The protector is not the enemy. The protector is the older sibling who stands in front of the younger one, fists up, ready to fight. It's not going to lower its fists until it knows the threat is gone.

What the protector is protecting

Underneath every distrustful part is a vulnerable part that has been hurt. Sometimes it's a child part that was betrayed by a caregiver. Sometimes it's a part that was told it was too much, too sensitive, too broken. Sometimes it's a part that tried to trust and was crushed.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link). Richard Schwartz, the creator of Internal Family Systems, makes it clear that there are no bad parts. Every part has a positive intention. Even the parts that seem destructive are trying to protect you in the only way they know how.

I once worked with a part that made me feel completely numb during therapy sessions. I couldn't feel my body. I couldn't access any emotions. I was just... blank. I thought this part was sabotaging my healing. But when I finally sat with it and asked what it was afraid of, it said something that broke my heart. It said, "If you feel that pain, you will die. I have to keep you numb to keep you alive."

Wild, right? That part wasn't trying to hurt me. It was trying to save my life. It just didn't know that I was no longer in that dangerous situation. It was still operating on old information. And it needed to be updated, not attacked.

For understanding how trauma fragments the self, a popular choice is Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link). Janina Fisher's work on structural dissociation explains exactly why our protectors are so vigilant. When trauma happens, parts of us get frozen in time. The protector part is stuck in the moment it had to step in. It doesn't know that years have passed. It doesn't know that you're safe now. It's still on guard duty.

How to work with the protector without triggering it

Here's what I've found works. And I want to be honest with you: this is not a quick fix. This is a slow, patient process. But it works.

First, I acknowledge the protector's presence. I say something like, "I notice there's a part of me that doesn't trust this. I see you. I know you're here." That's it. No demands. No expectations. Just acknowledgment.

Second, I thank the protector. "Thank you for keeping me safe. I know you've worked hard." This is often where the protector starts to soften. Because it's not used to being thanked. It's used to being ignored or criticized.

Third, I ask permission. "Can I spend some time with the part you're protecting? I promise I'll be gentle. And you can stay right here and watch." This is crucial. The protector needs to know it still has control. It needs to know it can intervene if things get too intense.

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery (paid link). Judith Herman's work reminds us that safety is the first stage of healing. You cannot do deep trauma work until the nervous system feels safe enough. The protector is not being difficult. It's enforcing the safety boundary that you need.

Notice what just happened in your body. Did you tense up when I mentioned working with the vulnerable part? Did you feel a resistance? That's the protector. It's doing its job. Thank it. Don't fight it.

I've learned to work with my protectors in small doses. Five minutes of parts work. Then a break. Then maybe another five minutes. The protector needs to see that I'm not going to overwhelm the system. It needs to see that I respect its limits.

What happens when the protector starts to trust

It doesn't happen all at once. It happens in moments. A slight relaxation in the shoulders. A deep breath that wasn't there before. A thought that says "maybe this time is different." And then the protector might step back, just a little, and let you see what's behind it.

And what's behind it is often a young part that is scared, sad, lonely, or ashamed. A part that has been waiting for someone to see it. A part that has been holding its breath for years, decades, a lifetime.

Sit with that. Imagine that part. Imagine how long it has been waiting. Imagine how much it has been carrying. And then imagine what it would feel like for that part to finally be seen, held, and loved.

That's what's possible when you approach your protectors with respect instead of resistance. That's what's possible when you stop trying to fix them and start trying to understand them.

I'm not going to tell you that this is easy. It's not. Some protectors are incredibly stubborn. Some have been on duty for so long they don't know how to rest. Some are so fused with your identity that you don't even recognize them as separate parts. But they are there. And they are waiting for you to see them.

Your healing is not about getting rid of the parts that don't trust. It's about building a relationship with them. It's about showing them, over and over, that you are safe now. That you can handle this. That they can finally, slowly, begin to rest.

And when they do? That's when the real healing begins.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Radical Acceptance (paid link) - A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection
  • No Bad Parts (paid link) - A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate
  • Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link) - For understanding how trauma fragments the self, a popular choice
  • Trauma and Recovery (paid link) - One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery
